---
title: "MAGEL2_interactome"
output: html_document
date: "2025-09-02"
---


Importing and converting known MAGEL2 interation partners
```{r}
# Install readxl if you haven't already
# install.packages("readxl")

# Load the required libraries
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)

# Import the data from the .xlsx file
file_path <- "MAGEL2_interactionPartners_2025.xlsx"
magel2_partners <- read_excel(file_path)

# Display the first few rows to confirm it loaded correctly
head(magel2_partners)

# Define the columns that have the long, descriptive format
cols_to_process <- c("MAGEL2_C-term", "MAGEL2_Full", "MAGEL2_Full_Stress", "MAGEL2_C-term_Stress")

# Clean the data
magel2_cleaned <- magel2_partners %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(cols_to_process), 
                # For each specified column, try to extract the gene name
                # If extraction fails (returns NA), keep the original value
                ~ coalesce(str_extract(., "(?<=GN=)[^ ]+"), .)))

# Display the first few rows of the cleaned data to verify
head(magel2_cleaned)


```
Define intersections of interest
```{r}
intersections_interactors <-
  list(
    #single
    "GFP.MAGEL2.2of3", 
    "GFP.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", 
    "BioID.MAGEL2.2of3",
    "BioID.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", 
    "MAGEL2_C-term", 
    "MAGEL2_Full",
    "MAGEL2_Full_Stress",
    "MAGEL2_C-term_Stress",
    "Hao et al",
    "Wijesuriya TM (2017)",
  
    #double overlaps
    c("GFP.MAGEL2.2of3", "GFP.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3"),
    c("BioID.MAGEL2.2of3","BioID.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3"),
    c("MAGEL2_C-term", "MAGEL2_Full"),
    c("MAGEL2_Full_Stress", "MAGEL2_C-term_Stress"),
    c("Hao et al", "Wijesuriya TM (2017)"),
      
    #quadruple overlaps
    c("GFP.MAGEL2.2of3", "GFP.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", "BioID.MAGEL2.2of3","BioID.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3"),
    c("MAGEL2_C-term", "MAGEL2_Full","MAGEL2_Full_Stress", "MAGEL2_C-term_Stress"),
    

    #BioID overlap
    c("BioID.MAGEL2.2of3", "BioID.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", "MAGEL2_C-term", 
    "MAGEL2_Full"),
    c("BioID.MAGEL2.2of3","BioID.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", "MAGEL2_C-term", "MAGEL2_Full","MAGEL2_Full_Stress", "MAGEL2_C-term_Stress"),
     c("BioID.MAGEL2.2of3","BioID.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", "MAGEL2_Full_Stress", "MAGEL2_C-term_Stress"),
    
    #eight overlap
    c("GFP.MAGEL2.2of3", "GFP.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", "BioID.MAGEL2.2of3","BioID.MAGEL2Q666P.2of3", "MAGEL2_C-term", "MAGEL2_Full","MAGEL2_Full_Stress", "MAGEL2_C-term_Stress")
)
    
    
    
```


```{r}
# --- 3. PREPARE DATA FOR UPSET PLOT ---

# Convert the data frame into a list of vectors, where each vector is a set of proteins
# Also, remove any NA values and duplicates within each set
protein_sets <- lapply(magel2_cleaned, function(col) unique(na.omit(col)))

# Convert the list of sets into a binary data frame suitable for the upset function
# This format has proteins as rows and sets as columns with TRUE/FALSE values
upset_data <- fromList(protein_sets)

# Get the names of all the sets (columns) for the plot
set_names <- colnames(magel2_cleaned)

# Convert the list into the binary data frame that UpSetR requires
upset_df <- fromList(protein_sets)

#upset(upset_df,
      #colnames(upset_df),
      #sort_sets = FALSE)


#upset(upset_df,
      #colnames(upset_df),
      #sort_sets = FALSE,
      #intersections = intersections_interactors,
      #sort_intersections=FALSE
      #)
```

List preparation for interaction map
```{r}
# --- 1. COMBINE AND CLEAN THE LISTS ---

# Define the columns you want to combine
columns_to_combine <- c("GFP.MAGEL2.2of3", "MAGEL2_Full", "Wijesuriya TM (2017)", "Hao et al")

# Use dplyr to select the columns, unlist them into a single vector,
# and then find the unique, non-NA proteins.
combined_protein_list <- magel2_cleaned %>%
  dplyr::select(all_of(columns_to_combine)) %>%
  unlist() %>%
  unique() %>%
  na.omit()

# --- 2. VIEW THE RESULT ---

# Print the first few proteins in your new combined list
cat("First 10 proteins in the combined list:\n")
print(head(combined_protein_list, 10))

# Print the total number of unique proteins
cat("\nTotal number of unique proteins in the list:", length(combined_protein_list), "\n")

combined_protein_list_df <- tibble(interactors = combined_protein_list)
```



MAGEL2 interactome combined with expression score from proteomics data
```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# Step 1: Setup - Install and Load Necessary Packages
# ==============================================================================
# This script uses several packages for data manipulation, network analysis,
# and visualization. If you haven't installed them yet, uncomment and run
# the line below.

# install.packages(c("tidyverse", "igraph", "readxl", "STRINGdb", "SummarizedExperiment", "RColorBrewer", "fields"))

# Load the libraries for this session
library(tidyverse)
library(igraph)
library(readxl)
library(STRINGdb)
library(SummarizedExperiment)
library(RColorBrewer) # For the RdBu color palette
library(fields)       # For the gradient legend

# ==============================================================================
# Step 2: Load and Process All Proteomics Data
# ==============================================================================
# This script assumes 'dep_8330_results_new' and 'dep_MGH_results_new'
# are already loaded in your R environment.

# --- Define the 6 comparisons vs. WT ---
comparisons <- c("DelC_vs_WT", "DupC_vs_WT", "HetDel_vs_WT",
                 "HomoDel_vs_WT", "PWSTI_vs_WT", "PWSTII_vs_WT")

# --- Function to extract and tidy data from a SummarizedExperiment object ---
tidy_proteomics_data <- function(se_object) {
  df <- as.data.frame(rowData(se_object)) %>%
    rownames_to_column("Protein")

  # Select the logFC (diff) and adjusted p-value columns
  diff_cols <- paste0(comparisons, "_diff")
  padj_cols <- paste0(comparisons, "_p.adj") # Use p.adj instead of significant

  df %>%
    dplyr::select(Protein, all_of(diff_cols), all_of(padj_cols)) %>%
    # Pivot Log2FC columns
    pivot_longer(
      cols = all_of(diff_cols),
      names_to = "comparison_diff",
      values_to = "Log2FC"
    ) %>%
    # Pivot p-value columns
    pivot_longer(
      cols = all_of(padj_cols),
      names_to = "comparison_padj",
      values_to = "PValue"
    ) %>%
    # Clean up comparison names and filter to keep matching pairs
    dplyr::mutate(
      comparison_diff = str_remove(comparison_diff, "_diff"),
      comparison_padj = str_remove(comparison_padj, "_p.adj")
    ) %>%
    dplyr::filter(comparison_diff == comparison_padj) %>%
    # Create the 'Significant' column based on the p-value cutoff
    dplyr::mutate(Significant = PValue < 0.05) %>%
    dplyr::select(Protein, Comparison = comparison_diff, Log2FC, Significant)
}


# --- Extract and combine data from both backgrounds ---
all_proteomics_data <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  tidy_proteomics_data(dep_8330_results_new),
  tidy_proteomics_data(dep_MGH_results_new)
)

# --- Calculate the Regulation Score for each protein ---
regulation_scores <- all_proteomics_data %>%
  # Keep only significantly changed results
  dplyr::filter(Significant == TRUE) %>%
  # Assign +1 for upregulation, -1 for downregulation
  dplyr::mutate(score = ifelse(Log2FC > 0, 1, -1)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(Protein) %>%
  # Sum the scores for each protein
  dplyr::summarise(RegulationScore = sum(score))

# ==============================================================================
# Step 3: Build Network and Map Regulation Score
# ==============================================================================

# --- Load the protein interactor list and get interactions from STRING ---
interactor_data <- read_excel("MAGEL2_interactors_input.xlsx")
protein_list <- combined_protein_list_df %>% dplyr::pull(1) %>% trimws() %>% unique()

string_db <- STRINGdb$new(version = "11.5", species = 9606, score_threshold = 700)
map_df <- data.frame(Protein = protein_list)
mapped_proteins <- string_db$map(map_df, "Protein", removeUnmappedRows = TRUE)
interactions <- string_db$get_interactions(mapped_proteins$STRING_id)

if (nrow(interactions) == 0) {
  stop("No interactions found. Try lowering the 'score_threshold'.")
}

edges <- interactions %>%
  dplyr::select(from_id = from, to_id = to, weight = combined_score) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(mapped_proteins, by = c("from_id" = "STRING_id")) %>%
  dplyr::rename(from_name = Protein) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(mapped_proteins, by = c("to_id" = "STRING_id")) %>%
  dplyr::rename(to_name = Protein) %>%
  dplyr::select(from = from_name, to = to_name, weight) %>%
  dplyr::filter(from != to, !is.na(from), !is.na(to))

# --- Create the network object ---
network <- graph_from_data_frame(d = edges, vertices = unique(mapped_proteins$Protein), directed = FALSE)
network <- igraph::simplify(network)
network <- delete.vertices(network, V(network)[degree(network) == 0])

# --- Join the regulation scores to the network nodes ---
node_df <- data.frame(Protein = V(network)$name) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(regulation_scores, by = "Protein") %>%
  # If a protein was never significant, its score is 0
  dplyr::mutate(RegulationScore = tidyr::replace_na(RegulationScore, 0))

V(network)$RegulationScore <- node_df$RegulationScore

# --- Find communities ---
set.seed(123)
communities <- cluster_fast_greedy(network)
V(network)$community <- communities$membership

# ==============================================================================
# Step 4: Print Community Summary Table
# ==============================================================================
# This new step creates and prints a summary table of proteins, their scores,
# and their assigned community.

community_summary <- data.frame(
  Protein = V(network)$name,
  Community = V(network)$community,
  RegulationScore = V(network)$RegulationScore
) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(Community, desc(RegulationScore))

print("--- Community Summary Table ---")
print(community_summary)


# ==============================================================================
# Step 5: Create Individual Plots for Each Community
# ==============================================================================

# --- Visualization Parameters ---
node_size <- 8
label_font_size <- 0.9
label_distance <- 1.5

# --- Create your specified color palette ---
original_brewer_palette <- brewer.pal(n = 10, name = "RdBu")

# Create a lighter subset by removing the darkest color from each end (the 1st and 10th)
lighter_brewer_subset <- original_brewer_palette[2:9]

# Create the new color ramp function using the lighter subset and then generate 100 colors
color_palette_adjusted <- colorRampPalette(rev(lighter_brewer_subset))(100)


# Set a fixed score limit to intensify colors.
# Scores outside this range will get the max/min color.
score_limit <- 12
color_breaks <- seq(-score_limit, score_limit, length.out = 101)


# --- Loop through each community and plot it ---
community_count <- length(unique(V(network)$community))

# --- Loop through each community and plot it ---
community_count <- length(unique(V(network)$community))

for (i in 1:community_count) {
  # Create a subgraph for the current community
  subgraph <- induced_subgraph(network, V(network)$community == i)
  
  # Skip if the community is too small
  if (gorder(subgraph) < 2) {
    next
  }
  
  # --- Assign colors to the subgraph nodes ---
  # Clamp scores to the fixed limit for color mapping
  scores_clamped <- V(subgraph)$RegulationScore
  scores_clamped[scores_clamped > score_limit] <- score_limit
  scores_clamped[scores_clamped < -score_limit] <- -score_limit
  
  node_colors <- color_palette_adjusted[cut(scores_clamped, breaks = color_breaks, include.lowest = TRUE)]
  node_colors[is.na(node_colors)] <- "grey" # Set nodes with no data to grey
  
  # --- NEW: Add transparency to the node colors ---
  # The alpha.f argument controls transparency (0=transparent, 1=opaque)
  node_colors_transparent <- adjustcolor(node_colors, alpha.f = 0.8)
  
  # --- Plot the subgraph ---
  set.seed(123)
  plot(
    subgraph,
    layout = layout_with_kk,
    vertex.color = node_colors_transparent, # Use the new transparent colors
    vertex.frame.color = NA,               # --- NEW: This removes the node outline ---
    vertex.size = node_size,
    vertex.label = V(subgraph)$name,
    vertex.label.cex = label_font_size,
    vertex.label.color = "black",
    vertex.label.dist = label_distance,
    edge.width = 1.5,
    edge.color = "lightgrey"
  )
  
  # --- Add Title and a true gradient Legend ---
  title(paste("Community", i, "- Regulation Score"), cex.main = 1.2)
  
  # Use image.plot from the 'fields' package to create a gradient legend
  image.plot(
    legend.only = TRUE,
    zlim = c(-score_limit, score_limit),
    col = color_palette_adjusted,
    legend.shrink = 0.4,
    legend.width = 0.8,
    legend.mar = 3,
    axis.args = list(at = c(-score_limit, 0, score_limit), labels = c(paste("<", -score_limit), 0, paste(">", score_limit)), cex.axis = 0.8),
    legend.args = list(text = "Regulation Score", side = 3, font = 2, cex = 0.8)
  )
}
```
Number of proteins
```{r}
# 1. Total unique proteins in your original mapping/data
total_mapped_proteins <- length(unique(mapped_proteins$Protein))

# 2. Total proteins included in the network object
# (This includes those with and without edges)
total_in_network <- vcount(network)

# 3. Proteins actually plotted 
# (Usually means proteins that have at least one connection/edge)
proteins_with_edges <- sum(degree(network) > 0)

# --- Print the Results ---
cat("Total proteins in original data: ", total_mapped_proteins, "\n")
cat("Proteins included in network object: ", total_in_network, "\n")
cat("Proteins actually plotted (with connections): ", proteins_with_edges, "\n")




# Get the names of proteins in the original list vs the network
original_names <- unique(mapped_proteins$Protein)
network_names <- V(network)$name

# Find proteins that are in the original list but NOT in the network
missing_proteins <- setdiff(original_names, network_names)

# View the missing list
print(missing_proteins)


```



Export list of MAGEL2 interaction partners
```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# EXPORT CLEANED MAGEL2 INTERACTION PARTNERS TO XLSX
# ==============================================================================

# Ensure the 'openxlsx' library is loaded
# install.packages("openxlsx") # Run this line if you don't have it installed
library(openxlsx)

# Define the output file name
output_file_name <- "MAGEL2_interactionPartners_cleaned.xlsx"

# Write the 'magel2_cleaned' data frame to the specified Excel file
write.xlsx(
  magel2_cleaned, 
  file = output_file_name,
  rowNames = FALSE, # Typically not needed for this kind of data
  overwrite = TRUE  # This will replace the file if it already exists
)

# Print a confirmation message to the console
message(paste("Successfully exported the cleaned data to:", output_file_name))
```



Export interactome communities with expression score
```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# Step 6: EXPORT NETWORK ANALYSIS RESULTS TO XLSX
# ==============================================================================

# Ensure the 'openxlsx' library is loaded
# install.packages("openxlsx") # Run this line if you don't have it installed
library(openxlsx)

# --- Create a named list where each element is a data frame for a sheet ---
# We will export the community summary table and the list of network edges.
list_for_export <- list(
  "Community_Summary" = community_summary,
  "Interaction_Edges" = edges
)

# --- Write the list to a single .xlsx file ---
write.xlsx(
  list_for_export, 
  file = "Network_Analysis_Summary.xlsx",
  rowNames = FALSE,
  overwrite = TRUE
)

# --- Print a confirmation message ---
message("Successfully exported network analysis results to 'Network_Analysis_Summary.xlsx'")
```
